title: Lemon drizzle loaf
subtitle: A sharp lemon loaf cake soaked with a sugar drizzle
excerpt: A sharp, close-crumbed loaf cake soaked while still hot with a lemon juice and sugar drizzle that forms a thin, crunchy crust as it dries. Makes one loaf, 8 to 10 slices.
sourceNotes: The lemon drizzle loaf in its current form is a twentieth-century British household cake, widely documented in British baking guides from the 1960s onwards. The technique of soaking a hot cake with syrup to produce a crisp surface has older roots in the syrup-soaked loaves recorded in Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, though the lemon-specific form is not among the named recipes. The recipe here follows the standard ratio widely reproduced in British home baking guides.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze before the drizzle is applied. Add the drizzle after thawing.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cake keeps well for up to 3 days in an airtight tin. The crust softens slightly after the first day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 180°C in a conventional oven

glossaryTerms.definition: A finishing technique where a mixture of sugar and citrus juice is poured over a hot cake as soon as it comes from the oven. The liquid soaks into the surface while the undissolved sugar crystals remain on top; as the cake cools, the sugar dries to a thin, slightly crunchy glaze. The cake must be hot for this to work: if it has already cooled, the syrup will sit on the surface rather than absorbing.

content.text: The drizzle is what makes this cake what it is. Pour it over the loaf the moment it comes out of the oven — still in the tin, before it has a chance to cool. The hot cake absorbs the juice; the caster sugar remains on the surface and dries to a thin crust. Cold cake will not absorb the syrup properly.

Preheat the oven to 170°C fan. Grease and line a 900 g loaf tin with baking paper. Beat the softened {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} (cake amount) together until pale and fluffy, about 4 minutes. Beat in the {{eggs}} one at a time, adding a tablespoon of flour with each egg to prevent curdling. Fold in the remaining {{self-raising-flour}} and the {{lemon}} zest, then stir in the {{whole-milk}} until the batter is smooth. Pour into the tin and level the surface.
Bake for 45–50 minutes, until golden and a skewer inserted into the centre comes out clean. The top will crack along the length, which is correct.

Mix the {{lemon}} juice (drizzle amount) with the {{caster-sugar}} (drizzle amount). Do not heat or stir until dissolved — you want some sugar crystals intact. The moment the cake comes out of the oven, use a skewer to poke holes across the surface, then pour the
over the top, making sure it reaches the edges. Leave to cool in the tin until the drizzle has set, then lift out onto a rack.
